one_line_takeaway: OTI is a training-free, human-interpretable heuristic (object-mask-weighted Sobel texture intensity) that reliably ranks which benign images are most vulnerable to transfer-based, ensemble, targeted, query-based, and cross-domain (medical segmentation) adversarial attacks, consistently beating random image selection by roughly 7-14 ASR points at low sampling rates, though it is only validated against random selection and its own ablated components, not against the prior model-dependent attackability measures it positions itself against.
key_finding: Ranking images by OTI and attacking only the lowest-OTI (most 'attackable') subset yields consistently higher ASR than attacking a random subset of the same size, across attack families, surrogate/target model combinations, and domains. The paper reports (all at sampling rate alpha=10%, averaged over target models): +13.39% ASR vs. random for single-surrogate untargeted transfer attacks; +6.79% ASR vs. random for single-surrogate targeted attacks; +13.55% ASR vs. random for ensemble-based untargeted attacks; +12.24% ASR vs. random for attacks on adversarially-trained (defended) target models. For query-based attacks (ADBA, TtBA), OTI-selected low-OTI images required smaller perturbation norms (L_inf and L2) to succeed. On the Kvasir-SEG segmentation task, low-OTI samples showed lower F1-scores after attack than high-OTI samples (qualitative visualization plus tabulated precision/F1/IoU across sampling rates). The ablation shows OTI (combined) outperforms both OAR-only and ITI-only, and OAR generally outperforms ITI alone; all three beat random selection, supporting the claim that object size and texture intensity are complementary attackability signals.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is Object Texture Intensity (OTI), a model-free, visually interpretable scalar measure of 'image attackability' -- how easily a given benign image can be turned into a successful adversarial example. OTI = (1/(C*H*W)) * ||object(x) ⊙ (f * x)||_1, i.e. the sum of absolute Sobel-filtered texture values ('image texture intensity', ITI) restricted (via Hadamard product) to a binarized semantic-object mask (yielding 'object area ratio', OAR). A lower OTI (small object, weak texture) is claimed to indicate a more attackable image. The authors state this is the first attackability measure requiring no proxy/surrogate model to be trained and the first to be visually interpretable (as opposed to prior gradient- or discriminator-based features), and they give two informal theoretical explanations: (1) a decision-boundary argument -- small objects/weak textures reduce the input margin, moving samples closer to the boundary; (2) a frequency-domain argument -- adversarial perturbations are believed to carry mid/high-frequency energy, so images with less semantically-relevant mid/high-frequency texture resist them less well.
threat_model: This is not a defense or attack paper in the classic sense; it is a sample-selection/evaluation tool for adversarial machine learning research. The implicit threat model is standard transfer-based / black-box and query-based evasion attacks against image classifiers (and one segmentation model): an attacker crafts adversarial perturbations on a surrogate/white-box model (e.g. ResNet-50, ViT-B/16, or ensembles) and transfers them to black-box target models, or queries a black-box target directly (decision-based/score-based attacks). OTI is used to pre-select which benign images from a pool are most 'attackable' before running the attack, under bounded perturbation budgets (L_inf = 10/255 untargeted, L_inf = 16/255 targeted, L_inf = 4/255 for the segmentation experiment). It is a research/evaluation instrument (useful for active learning, adversarial training subset selection, and efficient attack construction) rather than a proposed attack or defense mechanism itself.
limitations: The authors' own stated limitation (Conclusions section) is that OTI is restricted to image data, leaving analogous measures for audio, text, and other modalities as future work. Additional limitations evident from the text but not explicitly flagged by the authors: OTI still depends on an object-segmentation step (a saliency detector or ground-truth mask), so it is 'model-free' only with respect to the classifier/attack proxy, not with respect to needing a segmenter; the theoretical explanations (decision-boundary distance, mid/high-frequency perturbation energy) are argued qualitatively/illustratively (Figure 4) rather than derived or empirically validated with a controlled causal test; all reported results are from a single run on an A100 GPU with no variance/significance reporting; the segmentation-domain evaluation uses only 200 images and a single attack (MI-FGSM); no direct head-to-head numerical comparison against the two model-dependent baselines they critique (IAARS, ZGP) is reported anywhere in the paper -- only comparison against random sampling and against OTI's own sub-components (OAR, ITI).

datasets_used: ImageNet Validation set (50,000 images, full-scale classification)
quantitative_results: Single-surrogate untargeted transfer attacks (ANDA, GI-FGSM, BSR, SIA, ILPD, MA; R50/ViT-B/16 surrogates; L_inf=10/255): OTI-based sampling at alpha=10% improves average ASR by 13.39% over random sampling
Single-surrogate targeted attacks (Logit Margin, CFM, SU, FFT; R50 surrogate; L_inf=16/255): OTI-based sampling at alpha=10% improves average ASR by 6.79% over random sampling
Ensemble-based untargeted attacks (Ens, AdaEA, SMER, CWA; ensembles E1/E2/E3; L_inf=10/255): OTI-based sampling at alpha=10% improves average ASR by 13.55% over random sampling
Attacks on six adversarially-trained defended target models (R50 surrogate): OTI-based sampling at alpha=10% achieves 12.24% higher average ASR than random sampling

Query-based attacks (ADBA, TtBA; L_inf and L2 norms): OTI-selected low-OTI (more attackable) images require smaller perturbation norms to succeed than random selection (reported via Figure 9, no single aggregate percentage given)

Kvasir-SEG polyp segmentation (MI-FGSM attack, L_inf=4/255, U-Net-family targets): low-OTI samples show lower post-attack precision/F1/IoU than high-OTI samples across sampling rates 10%-100% (values reported per-figure, e.g. F1 examples 0.045-0.046 for selected vulnerable samples vs. 0.96-0.98 for resistant samples in the Figure 10 illustration)

Ablation: OTI outperforms both OAR-alone and ITI-alone across all six untargeted attack benchmarks and both surrogate models (R50, ViT-B/16); OAR alone generally outperforms ITI alone; all three (OTI, OAR, ITI) outperform random sampling
baselines_compared: Random sampling (alpha=100% equivalent) -- primary baseline across all main experiments
OAR (Object Area Ratio) alone -- ablation baseline
ITI (Image Texture Intensity) alone -- ablation baseline
